paragraphs: Beurre Bosc is a European pear that emerged in the early 1800s, with sources differing on whether it began in Belgium or France. It is named after the French horticulturist Louis Bosc, and Beurre means buttery in French, a nod to its rich texture.

It is easily recognised by its long tapering neck and warm cinnamon-brown russeted skin. The flesh is dense, crisp and sweet with a woodsy, spicy note, and because it holds its shape well when heated it is a favourite for baking, poaching and broiling as well as fresh eating. The season runs from autumn into spring.

claims.text: European pear that emerged in the early 1800s, in either Belgium or France
Named after French horticulturist Louis Bosc; Beurre means buttery
Distinctive long tapering neck and cinnamon-brown russeted skin
Dense, crisp, sweet flesh with a woodsy spicy flavour
Holds its shape when cooked, ideal for baking, poaching and broiling
Season runs from autumn into spring

paragraphs: Beurre Diel is a historic Belgian dessert pear from the Van Mons era. The chance seedling was found around 1800 at the Chateau of Perck near Vilvoorde by Meuris, head gardener to Dr Jean-Baptiste Van Mons, who named it after the German pomologist Augustin Diel. It is a large fruit with a yellowish green to orange skin marked by patches of russet and sometimes a faint flush.

The flesh is creamy white, melting and juicy, with a rich, sweet, aromatic flavour, though it can be a little gritty near the core and needs a warm season to ripen well. It is a mid season pear, harvested in October and used through to about December as a dessert fruit. The tree is a triploid and is self sterile, so it needs a compatible pollinator.

claims.text: Beurre Diel was found around 1800 near Vilvoorde, Belgium by Van Mons's gardener Meuris and named after the pomologist Diel.
The skin is yellowish green to orange with patches of russet, sometimes a slight flush, on a large fruit.
The flesh is creamy white, melting and juicy with a rich, sweet, aromatic flavour.
It is a triploid that is self sterile and needs a pollinator.

paragraphs: Beurre Easter, known in French as Doyenne d'Hiver and often called Easter Beurre, is an old pear of Belgian origin, traced to an aged tree at the Capucin monastery at Louvain that was recorded around 1823 as the Pastorale de Louvain. English and American authorities later settled on the name Easter Beurre, reflecting its very late season.

Its defining quality is keeping: it is one of the finest of the long storing pears, ripening in storage long after autumn pears are finished and lasting into April or May. The large fruit has melting, juicy flesh with a bright, vinous flavour, and unusually it will ripen in a cool cellar without special care.

claims.text: Beurre Easter, the French Doyenne d'Hiver, is of Belgian origin, traced to the Capucin monastery at Louvain around 1823.
It is also called Easter Beurre and Doyenne d'Hiver.
It is a very late, long keeping pear that ripens in storage and keeps into April or May.
The large fruit has melting, juicy flesh with a bright, vinous flavour.

sources.name: ChestOfBooks (horticultural journal): The Easter Beurre Pear
Promesse de Fleurs: Doyenne d'Hiver Pear

paragraphs: Beurre Hardy is a classic French dessert pear (Pyrus communis) raised around 1820 by M. Bonnet at Boulogne in northern France, and named after M. Hardy, the director of arboriculture at the Luxembourg Gardens in Paris. It reached England in the 1840s and has long been valued as one of the great traditional French butter pears.

Its standout feature is flavour: the buttery, melting, juicy flesh carries a distinctive rosewater or floral aroma that sets it apart from other pears. The medium to large, conical fruit has greenish-yellow skin heavily overlaid with a fine bronze russet. It ripens in mid-season. Beurre Hardy is generally grown with a pollinator rather than relied upon as self-fertile.

claims.text: Beurre Hardy was raised around 1820 by M. Bonnet at Boulogne, France, and named after M. Hardy, director of arboriculture at the Luxembourg Gardens, reaching England in the 1840s.
It is a European pear (Pyrus communis) and one of the traditional French butter pears.
Its buttery, melting, juicy flesh has a distinctive rosewater or floral aroma.
The medium to large conical fruit has greenish-yellow skin heavily covered with fine bronze russet.

sources.name: Specialty Produce: French Butter (Beurre Hardy) Pears
Trees of Antiquity: Beurre Hardy Pear

paragraphs: Beurre Superfin is a French dessert pear raised by M. Goubault at Mille Pieds near Angers in 1837, first fruiting around 1844. It is a medium to large fruit with a slightly rough, russet patched skin that ripens from mid green to yellow.

It is celebrated for flavour: the pale yellow flesh is melting, juicy and sweet with a delicate, scented, slightly tart character, ranking among the most outstanding flavoured pears. An autumn variety, it is picked in late September and keeps into October.

claims.text: Beurre Superfin was raised by M. Goubault near Angers, France in 1837, first fruiting around 1844.
The pale yellow flesh is melting, juicy and sweet with a delicate, scented, slightly tart flavour.
It is an autumn pear, picked late September and kept into October.

paragraphs: Chojuro is a classic Japanese nashi (Asian pear) that arose as a chance seedling in Japan around the 1890s. It is one of the russet nashi, with yellow to brownish, russeted skin rather than the smooth green or yellow of many other Japanese pears. The flesh is crisp and juicy but a little coarse in texture, and like all nashi it is eaten firm and crunchy, more like a crisp apple than a soft European pear.

Its standout quality is flavour: Chojuro is widely noted for a rich, sweet taste with distinctive butterscotch and caramel notes, a result of high sugar and low acidity. It ripens in late summer (around late August in the northern hemisphere) and was historically a very productive, disease-tolerant orchard variety, though its market share in Japan declined as smoother-skinned cultivars rose in popularity.

claims.text: Chojuro arose as a chance seedling in Japan around the 1890s
It is a russet nashi with yellow to brownish russeted skin
The flesh is crisp and juicy but a little coarse in texture
It is noted for a sweet flavour with butterscotch and caramel notes
It ripens in late summer, around late August
It was a productive, disease-tolerant variety whose Japanese market share later declined
Like all nashi it is eaten firm and crisp rather than soft

sources.name: Advances in Japanese pear breeding in Japan (Saito, PMC/NIH)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Pyrus pyrifolia 'Chojuro'
Gardener's Path: 9 of the Best Asian Pear Cultivars

paragraphs: Clapp's Favourite is an old American dessert pear raised by the Clapp family of Dorchester, Massachusetts in the mid 19th century and introduced around 1860. It is generally regarded as a cross of Bartlett and Flemish Beauty, and it resembles Bartlett in shape but ripens noticeably earlier, roughly a week to two weeks ahead of it.

The white flesh is fine grained, very juicy and sweet with a refreshing hint of acidity, making it a prized fresh eating pear. Its main quirk is poor keeping: the fruit should be picked while still firm and ripened off the tree, because left on the branch the core can break down, and ripe fruit lasts only a few days.

claims.text: Clapp's Favourite was raised by the Clapp family in Dorchester, Massachusetts and introduced around 1860.
It is generally considered a cross of Bartlett and Flemish Beauty.
It ripens earlier than Bartlett, with white, fine grained, juicy, sweet flesh.
It keeps poorly, so fruit is picked firm and ripened off the tree to avoid core breakdown.

paragraphs: Conference is an English dessert pear (Pyrus communis) raised by Thomas Francis Rivers at Rivers Nursery in Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire. Its name comes from the National British Pear Conference held in London in 1885, where it won first prize. It is by far the most widely grown pear in the United Kingdom, valued for reliable cropping and good scab resistance.

The fruit has a distinctive long, narrow, bottle shape with thick greenish-brown skin that is often heavily russeted, turning pale yellow when ripe. The flesh is fine, soft and sweet. Conference is unusually self-fertile for a pear and can set a crop on its own, though yields improve with a nearby pollinator such as Williams. It can also set seedless fruit without pollination.

claims.text: Conference was raised by Thomas Francis Rivers at Rivers Nursery in Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire, and named after the National British Pear Conference in London in 1885, where it won first prize.
It is a European pear (Pyrus communis) and the most widely grown pear in the UK, noted for scab resistance.
The fruit is long and bottle-shaped with greenish-brown, often russeted skin and fine, soft, sweet flesh.
Conference is unusually self-fertile and can crop on its own, but yields improve with a pollinator such as Williams.

paragraphs: Corella is a small Australian dessert pear (Pyrus communis) of the blush, or Forelle, type, sometimes called Australia's prettiest pear after the native parrot. It is grown widely in Australia, with early plantings associated with German settlers in South Australia's Barossa Valley.

Its appeal is its looks and eating quality at a small size: smooth green-yellow skin develops a striking rosy-red blush on the sunny side as it ripens. The flesh is creamy, fragrant, sweet and juicy with a slightly firm bite, making it popular eaten fresh, sliced into salads, or served with cheese.

claims.text: Corella is a small Australian blush (Forelle-type) Pyrus communis dessert pear, with early plantings linked to German settlers in the Barossa Valley
It has smooth green-yellow skin that develops a rosy-red blush, with creamy, fragrant, sweet, juicy, slightly firm flesh eaten fresh

paragraphs: Doyenne du Comice is a French dessert pear (Pyrus communis) raised in 1849 to 1850 at the Comice Horticole garden in Angers by the gardener Dhomme and Millet de la Turtaudiere. Widely regarded as one of the finest eating pears ever bred, it was famously praised in 19th century England, with the London Horticultural Journal calling it the best pear in the world in 1894. It has twice received the Royal Horticultural Society's Award of Garden Merit.

The large fruit is greenish-yellow, often with a red blush and some russet, ripening late in the season. Its flesh is pale, exceptionally sweet, melting, buttery and very juicy, which is the source of its reputation. The skin is delicate and bruises easily, so the fruit needs careful handling. It is not reliably self-fertile and is best grown with a compatible pollinator.

claims.text: Doyenne du Comice was raised in 1849 to 1850 at the Comice Horticole in Angers, France, by the gardener Dhomme and Millet de la Turtaudiere.

It is a European pear (Pyrus communis) celebrated as one of the finest dessert pears, called the best pear in the world by the London Horticultural Journal in 1894 and awarded the RHS Award of Garden Merit.
The large greenish-yellow fruit often carries a red blush and some russet, with pale, sweet, melting, buttery and very juicy flesh.
Its skin is delicate and bruises easily, requiring careful handling.

sources.name: Specialty Produce: Doyenne du Comice Pears

paragraphs: Duchesse d'Angouleme arose as a chance seedling in a garden near Angers, in the Maine-et-Loire region of France. The nurseryman M. Audusson began propagating it, and in 1820 he sent fruit to the Duchesse d'Angouleme seeking permission to name the pear in her honour.

It is one of the largest of the dessert pears, often attaining great size, with an irregular, bumpy surface. The thin skin ripens to a warm yellow netted with russet. When well grown the white flesh becomes buttery and melting, with a rich, sweet flavour.

claims.text: Duchesse d'Angouleme arose as a chance seedling in a garden near Angers, Maine-et-Loire, France.
The nurseryman M. Audusson propagated it and in 1820 sent fruit to the Duchesse d'Angouleme to gain permission to name it in her honour.
It is one of the largest dessert pears, with an irregular, bumpy surface.
The thin skin ripens to a warm yellow netted with russet.
When well grown the white flesh becomes buttery and melting, with a rich, sweet flavour.

sources.name: Slow Food Foundation Ark of Taste - Duchesse d'Angouleme Pear

paragraphs: Durondeau is a Belgian dessert pear raised in 1811 by M. Durondeau at Tongre-Notre-Dame, near Tournai. It is a handsome fruit with a golden to yellow green skin overlaid by red to brown russet and attractive lenticels, often with a flush on the sunny side.

The flesh is sweet and juicy with a pronounced, aromatic flavour carried by a little underlying acidity. It is an autumn pear, picked around late September and at its peak through October, with a short season. The tree has an upright, compact habit suited to small gardens and turns a fiery red in autumn.

claims.text: Durondeau is a Belgian pear raised in 1811 by M. Durondeau at Tongre-Notre-Dame near Tournai.
The skin is golden to yellow green with red to brown russet and a flush on the exposed side.
The flesh is sweet and juicy with an aromatic, fresh sweet sour flavour.
It is an autumn pear with a short season, picked late September and best in October.

paragraphs: Flemish Beauty is an old Belgian dessert pear (Pyrus communis) believed to descend from a chance seedling found in the woods of East Flanders around 1800, originally called Bosc Peer, or pear of the woods. The prolific Belgian breeder Jean-Baptiste Van Mons took up its propagation and renamed it Fondante des bois, loosely melt of the woods, under which name it became widely known across Europe. It later became one of the most common commercial pears in the United States.

The medium-sized fruit has yellow skin marked with russet, often with an attractive blush, and soft, buttery, juicy yellow flesh. The flavour is rich, sweet and notably musky, with a balance of sugar and acidity. For a European pear it is unusually self-fertile, though it crops more heavily with a pollinator. It is, however, quite susceptible to pear scab.

claims.text: Flemish Beauty descends from a chance seedling found in the woods of East Flanders, Belgium, around 1800, originally called Bosc Peer, and was renamed Fondante des bois by Jean-Baptiste Van Mons.
It is a European pear (Pyrus communis) that became one of the most common commercial pears in the United States.
The fruit has yellow, russeted skin and soft, buttery, juicy flesh with a rich, sweet, musky flavour.
For a European pear it is unusually self-fertile but is susceptible to pear scab.

sources.name: Silver Creek Nursery: Flemish Beauty European Pear

paragraphs: Flordahome is a low-chill pear developed by the University of Florida for warm, subtropical climates where most pears fail to get enough winter cold. It is a hybrid pear (a cross involving European Pyrus communis and Asian Pyrus pyrifolia) and needs only about 150 chill hours, making it one of the few pears that fruit reliably in mild-winter regions.

The fruit is soft-fleshed, sweet and juicy with smooth green to yellow skin, ripening early in summer (around July to August in Florida). It is good for fresh eating and canning, carries useful fire blight and leaf spot resistance, and is only partially self-fertile, so it crops better with a compatible pollinator such as Hood.

claims.text: Flordahome is a low-chill (about 150 chill hours) pear developed by the University of Florida, a Pyrus communis x Pyrus pyrifolia hybrid for warm climates
It ripens early (July to August), has soft sweet juicy flesh and green-yellow skin, is fire blight and leaf spot resistant, and is only partially self-fertile

sources.name: UF/IFAS: The Pear (Pyrus spp.) in Florida Home Gardens (HS1393)
Tree Vitalize: Pear Trees in Florida

paragraphs: Gin is an English perry pear (Pyrus communis) from west Gloucestershire, particularly the Newent area, first recorded as a vintage perry fruit in 1886. It bears heavy crops of small, almost round fruit, green with a slight orange or red flush at full ripeness.

It is grown for perry (pear cider) and is prized as a high-quality variety. Its name comes from the perry's aromatic, juniper-like character, which gives it a gin-like quality, with floral and herbal notes. The juice is classed as bittersharp, with medium acidity and medium tannin, and the variety is also valued for good resistance to scab and canker. It is sometimes blended with Blakeney Red.

claims.text: Gin is a west Gloucestershire perry pear from the Newent area, first recorded as a vintage fruit in 1886
It bears heavy crops of small, almost round fruit, green with a slight orange or red flush at ripeness
It is grown for perry and rated a high-quality variety
Its name reflects the perry's aromatic, juniper-like, gin character with floral and herbal notes
The juice is bittersharp with medium acidity and medium tannin
The variety has good resistance to scab and canker
It is sometimes blended with Blakeney Red

paragraphs: Glou Morceau was bred by the Abbe Hardenpont at Mons in Belgium around 1750, which is why it also goes by the name Beurre d'Hardenpont. It reached England in 1820 and became a prized Victorian dessert pear. The name translates roughly as 'delicious morsel'.

This is a late season pear with fine, melting, juicy flesh, very sweet and richly flavoured, though it needs warm conditions to ripen fully. The fruit is medium to large and oval to pear-shaped, with smooth greenish-yellow skin carrying very little russet. It hangs well on the tree and keeps into winter.

claims.text: Glou Morceau was bred by the Abbe Hardenpont at Mons, Belgium, around 1750.
It is also known as Beurre d'Hardenpont.
It reached England in 1820.
The name translates as 'delicious morsel'.
The flesh is fine, melting and juicy, very sweet and rich, but needs warm conditions to develop fully.
It is a late season pear that hangs well on the tree and keeps into winter.
The fruit is medium to large, oval to pear-shaped, with smooth greenish-yellow skin and very little russet.

paragraphs: Green Horse is a very old English perry pear (Pyrus communis) long associated with the orchards of Gloucestershire and the Three Counties, recorded in the 1800s. The fruit is round and green to yellowish green, sometimes with a light orange flush, often carrying russet around the stem and eye spreading onto the cheeks.

It is grown chiefly for perry (pear cider), where it is rated a best-quality variety producing a high-acid, low-tannin juice. It ripens in mid to late autumn (around October in England) on a large, vigorous, reliable-cropping tree. Beyond perry, the hard fruit has historically also been stewed or pickled.

claims.text: Green Horse is a very old Gloucestershire perry pear recorded in the 1800s
The fruit is round and green to yellowish green, sometimes with a light orange flush, with russet around the stem and eye
It is grown chiefly for perry and is rated a best-quality variety
It produces a high-acid, low-tannin perry
It ripens in mid to late autumn, around October in England
The fruit can also be stewed or pickled
It grows into a large, vigorous, reliable-cropping tree

sources.name: National Perry Pear Centre: Green Horse
Slow Food Foundation Ark of Taste: Green Horse Perry Pear

paragraphs: Red Longdon is a traditional English perry pear (Pyrus communis) from the Three Counties region of England. The fruit is turbinate to pyriform, green or yellowish green with a strong red or red-orange flush spreading from the eye and russet around the stem, a colouring that gives the variety its name. In Australia the same variety is grown as Henry's Red Longdon, after Henry of Harcourt near Bendigo, where perry pears were introduced during the gold rush from 1850.

It is grown for perry (pear cider) rather than fresh eating, producing a good-quality perry of medium acidity and medium tannin. It is a vigorous, scab-resistant, midseason-harvesting tree. The Henry's Red Longdon name was adopted in Australia partly because no original index tree survived for direct comparison.

claims.text: Red Longdon is a traditional English perry pear from the Three Counties region
The fruit is turbinate to pyriform, green or yellowish green with a strong red or red-orange flush from the eye and russet around the stem
In Australia it is grown as Henry's Red Longdon, after Henry of Harcourt near Bendigo
Perry pears were introduced to Harcourt during the Bendigo gold rush from 1850
It is grown for perry, producing a good-quality perry of medium acidity and medium tannin
It is a vigorous, scab-resistant tree

sources.name: National Perry Pear Centre: Red Longdon
Slow Food Foundation Ark of Taste: Red Longdon Perry Pear

paragraphs: Hood is a very low-chill pear released by the University of Florida for warm, subtropical growing where winters are too mild for traditional European pears. Needing only a low number of chill hours, it is one of the standard low-chill choices for Florida and similar climates, and it is interfruitful with Flordahome as a pollinating partner.

The fruit is large and early, ripening in mid-summer, with yellow-green skin and soft, sweet, mild flesh often compared to a Bartlett. It also carries moderate fire blight resistance, a key trait for humid, warm-climate orchards.

claims.text: Hood is a very low-chill pear released for warm climates by the University of Florida, interfruitful with Flordahome and with moderate fire blight resistance
It produces large, early fruit ripening mid-summer with yellow-green skin and soft, sweet, mild Bartlett-like flesh

sources.name: UF/IFAS: The Pear (Pyrus spp.) in Florida Home Gardens (HS1393)
Tree Vitalize: Pear Trees in Florida

paragraphs: Jargonelle is one of the oldest pear varieties known, thought to have originated in the former French region of Anjou and first recorded in England by John Parkinson in 1629. Its hardiness, longevity and reliable cropping saw it planted widely throughout northern Britain.

It is one of the earliest summer pears to ripen. The fruit is medium-sized and long conical, with greenish-yellow skin flushed brownish-red and dotted with small russet patches. The pale yellow flesh is tender and juicy with a very sweet, slightly musky flavour. It is a tip bearer, so it is not suited to training as cordons or espaliers.

claims.text: Jargonelle is one of the oldest pear varieties, thought to have originated in Anjou, France.
It was first recorded in England by John Parkinson in 1629.
Its hardiness, longevity and reliable cropping led to it being planted throughout northern Britain.
It is one of the earliest summer pears to ripen.
The fruit is medium-sized and long conical, with greenish-yellow skin flushed brownish-red and small russet patches.
The flesh is pale yellow, tender and juicy with a very sweet, slightly musky flavour.
It is a tip bearer and not suitable for training into cordons or espaliers.

paragraphs: Josephine de Malines is an old Belgian winter pear (Pyrus communis) raised in the early 1800s by Major Pierre Joseph Esperen at Malines (Mechelen), who named it after his wife, Josephine. It is a late, long-keeping dessert pear, traditionally picked in autumn for eating through winter once it has mellowed in store.

The flesh is the draw: rich, buttery and melting, sweet and perfumed, often rated among the finest-flavoured of all pears. The small to medium fruit are conical and greenish-yellow, sometimes with a dull red flush. It is not self-fertile, so it needs a compatible pollinator nearby.

claims.text: Josephine de Malines is a Belgian winter pear raised in the early 1800s by Major Pierre Joseph Esperen at Malines (Mechelen), named after his wife Josephine
It is a late, long-keeping pear with rich, buttery, melting, perfumed flesh, greenish-yellow fruit sometimes with a red flush, and needs a pollinator

paragraphs: Kosui is a Japanese nashi (Asian pear) bred from a cross of Kikusui and Wasekozo, released in Japan in 1959 from the national pear breeding program. It produces medium-sized, rounded fruit largely covered in golden-brown russet, on a background ranging from light green to bronze. The name Kosui means good water, a nod to its juiciness.

The flesh is soft for a nashi yet crisp and very juicy, with a fine texture and a sweet, low-acid flavour. Kosui is an early-ripening variety, maturing in mid to late summer ahead of many other nashi, and for decades it was one of the most widely grown pears in Japan. Like other nashi it is eaten firm and crunchy rather than ripened soft.

claims.text: Kosui originated from a cross between Kikusui and Wasekozo and was released in 1959
The fruit is medium-sized and rounded, largely covered with golden-brown russet
The name Kosui means good water
The flesh is soft yet juicy with a very fine texture and sweet, low-acid flavour
It is an early-ripening nashi, maturing ahead of many other varieties
It was for decades one of the principal pear cultivars grown in Japan
Like other nashi it is eaten firm and crunchy

sources.name: Advances in Japanese pear breeding in Japan (Saito, PMC/NIH)
Gardener's Path: 9 of the Best Asian Pear Cultivars

paragraphs: Mirandino Rosso is an old Italian summer pear from the Mantua district of northern Italy, where it was recorded in the hunting reserves of the Guerrini Gonzaga marquises near Moglia from the 1700s. The same pear is known as Bella di Giugno and as Precoce di Moglia, the latter pointing to its place of origin, with Mirandino Rosso the name favoured in Emilia Romagna. It ranks among the earliest of all pears.

The fruit is small and typically piriform, with a yellow skin that flushes red on the parts most exposed to the sun. The flesh is white, melting and fairly juicy, with a sweet, aromatic character. Picking begins from the last third of June, so it is eaten fresh in the height of summer rather than stored. The Book of Pears lists it simply as an Italian summer pear.

claims.text: Old Italian summer pear from the Mantua district, recorded in the Guerrini Gonzaga hunting reserves near Moglia from the 1700s.
Also known as Bella di Giugno and Precoce di Moglia, with Mirandino Rosso the name used in Emilia Romagna.
Small piriform fruit with yellow skin flushed red where exposed to the sun.
White, melting, fairly juicy flesh with a sweet, aromatic flavour.
Very early pear, picked from the last third of June and eaten fresh in summer.
Listed in The Book of Pears as an Italian summer pear.

sources.name: The Book of Pears (Fruit Forum directory)

paragraphs: Moorcroft is a traditional English perry pear (Pyrus communis) believed to have originated at Moorcroft Farm in Colwall, in the Malvern Hills, in the early 1800s. East of the River Severn it is widely known as Malvern Hills, and it carries several other names including Malvern Pear, Choke Pear and Choker. It is famously linked to the cheese Stinking Bishop, which is washed in perry made from this pear and named after a former Moorcroft Farm occupant.

As its Choke Pear names suggest, the fruit is not for eating fresh but for perry (pear cider). It is regarded as a fine vintage perry variety, yielding a juice of medium acidity and medium tannin, with little or no citric acid, that ferments to a good-quality perry. It grows into a large, long-lived, oak-like tree.

claims.text: Moorcroft is believed to have originated at Moorcroft Farm in Colwall, in the Malvern Hills, in the early 1800s
East of the Severn it is known as Malvern Hills, with other names including Malvern Pear, Choke Pear and Choker
It is linked to Stinking Bishop cheese, which is washed in perry from this pear and named after a former Moorcroft Farm occupant
The fruit is grown for perry, not for eating fresh
It is regarded as a fine vintage perry variety giving a medium acid, medium tannin juice
It grows into a large, oak-like tree

paragraphs: Nijisseiki, whose name means 20th Century, is a Japanese nashi or Asian pear (Pyrus pyrifolia), not a European pear. It traces to a chance seedling discovered on a rubbish heap in Japan in the late 1890s, and went on to become one of the most popular and widely grown Asian pear cultivars in Japan and the United States. Unlike the soft, buttery European pears, nashi are eaten crisp and apple-like straight from the tree.

The fruit is round and apple-shaped with smooth, thin, yellow to greenish-yellow skin. Its white flesh is clean, crisp, very juicy and sweet, the qualities that made it famous. It ripens in late summer. As a smooth-skinned yellow nashi it is more prone to fire blight than russeted types, and at least two compatible cultivars are usually needed for reliable pollination.

claims.text: Nijisseiki means 20th Century and is a Japanese nashi (Asian pear, Pyrus pyrifolia) that arose from a chance seedling found on a rubbish heap in Japan in the late 1890s.
It is one of the most popular Asian pear cultivars, eaten crisp and apple-like rather than soft like European pears.
The round fruit has smooth yellow to greenish-yellow skin and white flesh that is crisp, very juicy and sweet, ripening in late summer.
As a smooth yellow nashi it is more susceptible to fire blight than russeted types and usually needs at least two cultivars for pollination.

sources.name: Victoriana Nursery: Nijisseiki Asian Pear (Pyrus pyrifolia)

paragraphs: Packham's Triumph is a famous Australian-bred pear (Pyrus communis), raised in 1896 by orchardist Charles Packham at Molong in the New South Wales central west, as a cross between the old English variety Uvedale's St Germain and Williams' Bon Chretien. Packham proposed the name Packham's Seedling, but the agriculture department suggested Packham's Triumph. It went on to become Australia's leading commercial and export pear.

The fruit has a classic pear shape with thin green skin (often lightly russeted) that fades to pale yellow as it ripens. The ivory flesh is fine-grained, juicy and creamy with a sweet, mildly musky, aromatic flavour, ripening softly rather than staying crisp.

claims.text: Packham's Triumph is an Australian pear bred in 1896 by Charles Packham at Molong, NSW, from a cross of Uvedale's St Germain and Williams' Bon Chretien, and named Packham's Triumph by the agriculture department
It has thin green skin with some russet fading to pale yellow, and fine-grained, juicy, creamy, sweet, mildly musky flesh

sources.name: Australian Dictionary of Biography: Charles Henry Packham
Australian Food Timeline: Packham pear developed in NSW

paragraphs: The Paradise pear traces back to Sicily, Italy, and has since been taken up in Australia. It is also sold under the names Faccia Bella (Italian for beautiful face), sugar pear and crystal pear, and is classed as a cocktail pear for its very small size, roughly that of a golf ball. The fruit is squat and round with smooth skin that shifts from green to gold with a red blush as it ripens.

Inside, the white flesh is crisp, moist and juicy, staying firm and crunchy even when fully ripe, with a sweet, fragrant taste. The small size suits cheese plates, salads and lunch boxes, and the fruit also takes well to poaching, baking, preserving and pickling. In Australia it is an early-season pear, ripening around January to February.

claims.text: The Paradise pear originated in Sicily, Italy, and is now grown in Australia.
It is also known as Faccia Bella, sugar pear and crystal pear.
It is a cocktail pear, small and round, about the size of a golf ball, with skin that turns from green to gold with a red blush.
The white flesh is crisp, moist and juicy, staying firm and crunchy even when fully ripe, with a sweet flavour.
It is used fresh in salads and on cheese plates and also for cooking, preserving and pickling.
In Australia it is an early-season pear ripening around January to February.

paragraphs: Red Sensation (also sold as Sensation or Williams Red) is a red sport of the Williams pear that arose in Australia around 1940. It is one of several red mutant clones derived from Williams by spontaneous mutation, alongside Max Red Bartlett and Rosired. The fruit ripens from yellow green to an all over ruby red, giving it the showy skin that sets it apart from the parent.

Because it is a Williams sport, it shares the parent's eating qualities: melting, juicy, creamy white flesh with a fine texture. It is an early season pear, harvested in summer (around February in Australia). The tree also carries striking red tinted foliage, adding ornamental value to its dual role as a dessert pear.

claims.text: Red Sensation is a red skinned sport of the Williams pear that originated in Australia around 1940.
The skin ripens from yellow green to an all over red, and it is also known as Sensation or Williams Red.
As a Williams sport it shares the parent's melting, juicy flesh and ripens early in the season.

paragraphs: San Giovanni is a very old Italian summer pear. Its name refers to the feast day of Saint John (San Giovanni) in late June, the time when the fruit ripens.

It is an early ripening variety bearing small, pear-shaped fruit with a pale, light yellow-green skin. The white flesh is firm and juicy with a pleasant aromatic flavour. Like most early summer pears it does not store well and is best eaten fresh.

claims.text: San Giovanni is a very old Italian summer pear.
Its name refers to the feast day of Saint John (San Giovanni) in late June, when the fruit ripens.
It is an early ripening variety, ripening towards the end of June.
The fruit is small and pear-shaped (pyriform) with a pale light yellow-green skin.
The white flesh is firm and juicy with a pleasant aromatic flavour.

sources.name: The Book of Pears (Fruit Forum) - San Giovanni
Verde Commerce - Pero San Giovanni (Pyrus communis)

paragraphs: Shinseiki, whose name means new century, is a Japanese nashi (Asian pear) released in 1945 from the Okayama prefecture breeding program, with Nijisseiki (Twentieth Century) in its parentage. Unlike the russet nashi such as Chojuro, Shinseiki is a smooth-skinned, bright pale-yellow pear, round to slightly flattened in shape.

The white flesh is crisp, very juicy and sweet with a slight tartness, and it is eaten firm like other nashi. It is an early to mid-season variety, ripening around late August, and is valued in warm-winter regions for its low chilling requirement and reliable, precocious cropping. Several sources describe it as self-fertile, though some recommend a second cultivar nearby for best fruit set.

claims.text: Shinseiki means new century and was released in 1945 from the Okayama prefecture breeding program
It has Nijisseiki (Twentieth Century) in its parentage
It is a smooth-skinned, bright pale-yellow pear, round to slightly flattened
The white flesh is crisp, juicy and sweet with a slight tartness
It ripens around late August
It is valued in warm-winter regions for its low chilling requirement and precocious cropping
It is described as self-fertile, though some sources advise a second cultivar for best set

sources.name: Advances in Japanese pear breeding in Japan (Saito, PMC/NIH)
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Pyrus pyrifolia 'Shinseiki'
Gardener's Path: 9 of the Best Asian Pear Cultivars
Fedco Seeds: Shinseiki (New Century) Asian pear

paragraphs: Williams (full name Williams' Bon Chretien) is the classic European pear (Pyrus communis), thought to have arisen around 1765 to 1770 in the garden of a schoolmaster named John Stair at Aldermaston, England, and later spread by a nurseryman named Williams. In the United States and Canada the same variety is known as Bartlett, after Enoch Bartlett, who sold it without knowing its origin. The two were not recognised as identical until 1828.

Its flavour is so characteristic it is often called the definitive pear taste: aromatic and musky, with white to pale-yellow flesh that ripens soft, juicy and sweet. The skin turns from green to yellow as it ripens. Williams is hugely versatile, eaten fresh, widely canned, and distilled into the pear brandy Poire Williams.

claims.text: Williams (Williams' Bon Chretien) is a Pyrus communis European pear that arose around 1765 to 1770 at Aldermaston, England, and is known as Bartlett in the US and Canada, the two not recognised as the same until 1828
It has aromatic, musky flesh that ripens soft, juicy and sweet with skin turning green to yellow, and is used fresh, canned, and for Poire Williams brandy

paragraphs: Winter Cole is an Australian heritage pear, a seedling of the Belgian winter keeper Winter Nelis raised by J.C. Cole at Richmond, Victoria, and introduced by the nurseryman J. Brunning and Sons in the late 1880s. Like its parent it is a European pear (Pyrus communis) rather than an Asian nashi, and it inherits Winter Nelis's reputation for late ripening and long storage, holding well into winter.

It is a medium-sized, cool-climate dessert pear with green skin marked by brown russet, and flesh that is juicy and rich in flavour. As a late-season variety it is valued as a keeper, picked in late autumn and eaten through the colder months. It is not self-fertile and crops best alongside other European pears such as Beurre Bosc, Williams or Doyenne du Comice.

claims.text: Winter Cole is a seedling of Winter Nelis raised by J.C. Cole at Richmond, Victoria, and introduced by J. Brunning and Sons in the late 1880s.
Its parent Winter Nelis is a European pear (Pyrus communis) and a late-season variety prized for long storage into winter.
Winter Cole has green, russeted skin and juicy, richly flavoured flesh, and is a late-season keeper.
It is not self-fertile and crops best with other European pear pollinators.

sources.name: Heritage Fruit Trees: Winter Cole Pear

paragraphs: Winter Nelis is an old Belgian late pear (Pyrus communis) raised from seed in the early 1800s by the amateur horticulturist Jean-Charles de Nelis, near Brussels, and also known as Bonne de Malines. Picked in late autumn, it is one of the better-keeping pears, storing for a couple of months for use through winter.

Despite its modest, often russeted skin (which kept it from large commercial production), it is prized for eating quality: yellowish, fine-grained flesh that is buttery and melting, with a rich, sugary, vinous flavour and fine aroma. The small to medium fruit suits both fresh eating and cooking.

claims.text: Winter Nelis is a Belgian late pear (also called Bonne de Malines) raised from seed in the early 1800s by Jean-Charles de Nelis, and stores well into winter
It has russeted skin and yellowish, fine-grained, buttery, melting flesh with a rich, sugary, vinous flavour, used fresh and cooked

paragraphs: Ya Li is a Chinese white pear, a cultivar of Pyrus x bretschneideri native to north China and grown there for centuries. Its name means duck pear, after a shape that, unusually for an Asian pear, is elongated and narrow toward the stem like a European pear rather than round like a typical nashi.

Like other Asian pears it eats crisp rather than soft and buttery: the white to pale-yellow flesh is very juicy and crunchy, often compared to a mild Bosc but with higher water content and lower sugar. It is eaten fresh while firm, prized for its refreshing, watery crunch.

claims.text: Ya Li is a cultivar of the Chinese white pear Pyrus x bretschneideri, native to north China, whose name means duck pear and which is elongated like a European pear rather than round like a typical nashi
It has crisp, very juicy white to pale-yellow flesh compared to a mild Bosc but with higher water content and lower sugar, eaten fresh while firm
